formula: SiO2 (with trace Ti, Al, Mn or microscopic inclusions)
_all_text: Rose Quartz rose-quartz SiO2 (with trace Ti, Al, Mn or microscopic inclusions) Rose quartz is unusual among quartz varieties in being almost always massive (not crystalline). Well-formed pink quartz crystals (sometimes called 'crystalline rose quartz' or 'pink quartz') may have a different color mechanism (Al-P centers). The fibrous inclusions responsible for asterism in star rose quartz are rutile needles. Color fades on prolonged exposure to direct sunlight. Rose quartz has a characteristic soft, diffuse, milky-pink quality from the light-scattering fibrous inclusions. It is never facet-grade transparent in classic form. The color is gentle and warm — not vivid like ruby or pink tourmaline but consistently soft. Star rose quartz cut as cabochon shows the six-rayed star most vividly under a point light source. Rose quartz has one of the most robust modern metaphysical followings of any mineral. Ancient Assyrian and Roman cosmetic recipes include rose quartz facial rollers — a practice that has seen a massive modern revival. Egyptian legend held that rose quartz was a gift from Aphrodite (via Eros) to humanity — used to prevent aging. The association with love and the heart is essentially universal in modern crystal culture. unconditional love compassion and gentleness femininity and nurturing heart chakra beauty and Venus Carved into beads and figurines in ancient Egypt and Rome Facial masks in ancient Egyptian burials (rose quartz) Carving material — spheres, eggs, hearts (modern) rose quartz pink quartz love stone Venus stone heart chakra crystal star rose quartz rose quartz sphere facial roller Valentine mineral silicate variety quartz pink massive love-stone trigonal pegmatite wellness-market high-priority universal-love-stone Venus-blush diffuse-pink-warmth feminine-archetype heart-mineral pale-pink rose-pink
